Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
v
Preface
The Physical Education and Sport Teaching Module for Form 1 has been developed in 
response to the introduction of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC), a transformative 
initiative aimed at enhancing quality education and relevance.
This teaching module is designed to support teachers to effectively deliver Physical Education 
and Sport lessons under the new curriculum. Its primary objectives are to, bridge the gap created 
by the absence of approved textbooks and equip teachers with practical skills and assessments 
tailored to learners’ needs and levels.
The Ministry of Education acknowledges the challenges associated with transitioning to a new 
curriculum. Consequently, this module reflects our commitment to providing teachers with 
structured guidance, suggested activities, formative and summative assessments aligned with 
the CBC’s objectives.
While this module serves as a foundational resource, we encourage teachers to supplement it 
with their own research and innovations to address potential gaps during implementation. The 
suggested activities and assessments are designed to foster practical and critical thinking skills 
in learners, ensuring relevance and adaptability across diverse school contexts.
Teachers are encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their unique classroom dynamics and 
explore alternative approaches when necessary. We hope this module will empower teachers 
to navigate the new competence-based curriculum with confidence, inspiring and equipping 
learners with applied knowledge, skills, and values.
Kelvin Mambwe  (PhD)
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Physical Education and Sport

Charles Ndakala (Dr.)
Director - Curriculum Development

Terminology

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
viii
INTRODUCTION 
The Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) is designed to equip learners with competences 
necessary to navigate the complexities of the modern world. This Physical Education and Sport 
(PES) teaching module is tailored to support teachers in implementing the CBC, focusing on 
the attainment of specific competences of specified sub-topics as indicated in the syllabus, but 
aligned to the general competences like creativity and innovation, critical thinking, problem-
solving, and effective communication.
Each specific competence is supported by carefully designed learning activities that cater for 
diverse learning styles and abilities. These learning activities further give guidance on how they 
should be effectively administered to the learners to meet the expected standards that speak to the 
specific competences. Furthermore, this module encourages teachers to assess learner progress 
using a range of strategies.
By using this PES teaching module, teachers will be able to support learners to acquire competences 
outlined in the syllabus. In turn, learners will develop practical skills and enhancement of talents, 
creativity, self-expression and a sense of aesthetics.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
1
TOPIC: 1.5 FITNESS ACTIVITIES
INTRODUCTION
Fitness goes beyond physical abilities like sit-ups or running. It’s about the body’s systems 
working efficiently to meet every day’s demands and adapt to stress. Fitness connects closely 
to developing overall well-being. It includes physical, social, emotional, spiritual, and mental 
aspects that enable a learner to participate in various activities and sports effectively. Health 
and fitness are intertwined, emphasizing the importance of being fit enough to handle life’s 
challenges, whether academic or athletic. This perspective teaches learners that total fitness 
contributes to success in both sports and daily life.
World over, Zambia inclusive, activities such as aerobics, jogging and marathons demonstrate 
a growing emphasis on health and fitness. However, the prevalence of sedentary lifestyles and 
extended leisure time has led to health problems like heart disease, obesity and some cancers, 
while also negatively impacting fitness levels.
This unit introduces the learners to the fundamental concepts and practices of fitness activities 
in Physical Education and Sport. Learners will understand and explore the importance of health 
related components, Isotonic and isometric activities. It will further explore various forms of 
exercise, ranging from heart-pumping cardiovascular workouts to muscle-building strength 
training activities.  
Learners should acquire the following general competencies:
Communication: Ability to follow instructions, provide feedback, and communicate with peers 
during fitness activities.
Critical thinking: Ability to evaluate the effectiveness of different fitness activities, consider 
safety guidelines, and make informed decisions about one’s own fitness.
Problem-solving: Ability to adapt to new fitness activities, overcome challenges, and find 
solutions to problems that arise during participation.
Emotional intelligence: Ability to take responsibility for one’s own fitness, set goals, and 
monitor progress.
Teamwork: 	 Ability to work collaboratively with peers during fitness activities, promoting 
mutual support and encouragement.
HOOK
What role do you think fitness activities play in maintaining a healthy lifestyle and how can you 
incorporate Physical Education into your daily routine?
TERMINOLOGY
Aerobic exercise: Physical activity that raises the heart rate and improves cardiovascular health.
Body Composition: The proportion of body fat to lean body mass.
Cardiovascular Endurance: The ability of the heart, lungs, and blood vessels to supply oxygen

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
2
to the muscles during prolonged physical activity.
Dynamic Movement: Movement that involves the contraction and relaxation of muscles to 
move the joints.
Fitness: The ability to perform physical activity with efficiency and effectiveness.
Flexibility exercise: Physical activities designed to improve the range of motion of muscles and 
joints.
Flexibility: The range of motion in joints and muscles.
Isometric Contraction: occurs when a muscle generates force without changing its length 
resulting in no movement at a joint.
Isotonic Contraction: occurs if a muscle changes length while generating force resulting in 
movement at a joint.
Concentric contraction: The muscle shortens as it contracts (e.g., lifting a weight). 
Eccentric contraction: The muscle lengthens as it contracts (e.g., lowering a weight).
Muscle Contraction: The process by which muscles generate force to move the joints.
Muscular Endurance: The ability of muscles to sustain activity over time.
Muscular Strength: The ability of muscles to exert force against resistance.
Strength training: Physical activity that improves muscular strength and endurance.
Warm-up: A variety of simple activities that are designed to prepare the body for either moderate 
or complex activities.
Sub-Topic 1.5.1 Health-Related Components of Fitness
Introduction
This subtopic focuses on the five health-related components of fitness that are essential for 
maintaining overall physical well-being. These components are Cardiovascular Endurance, 
Muscular Strength, Muscular Endurance, Flexibility and Body Composition. Learners need 
to realise the importance of each health-related component. They should be able to identify 
exercises and activities that improve each component and also recognise the benefits of regular 
physical activity on overall health and well-being.
Specific Competence(s): 
1.5.1.1 Perform health related activities
TIP
The lesson on health related fitness may be conducted in the classroom. The teacher can also 
teach the learners from any of the environments such as Natural (playing grounds…), Artificial 
(Modified classroom…) and Technological (video clips, shows...). 
Teaching material/Resources
Stop watch or mobile phone timer, low bench, meter rule or measuring tape, yoga mat, chalk or 
marker, string, (local materials such as bricks, plastic bottles, old tires, ropes…)…

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
3
 
 Learning Activity:
 Identifying health-related activities (Endurance, body composition. Flexibility, mus­
cular strength…).
DISCUSSION
Learners should: 
Task 1;
In groups identify and discuss health related fitness activities.
 
CONTENT TIP
 
 Health Related Fitness refer to the aspects of physical fitness that is directly related to 
an individual’s overall health and well-being.
 
 An athlete should maintain good health to perform activities with ease and still have 
sufficient energy to handle forth coming activities.
Components of Health-Related Fitness 
1.	 Cardiovascular Endurance: The ability of the heart, lungs, and blood vessels to supply 
oxygen to the muscles during sustained physical activity.
2.	 Muscular Strength: The ability of muscles to generate force against resistance.
3.	 Muscular Endurance: The ability of muscles to sustain activity over time.
4.	 Flexibility: The range of motion in joints and muscles.
5.	 Body Composition: The proportion of body fat to lean body mass.
TIP
  
    S/N
COMPONENTS 
ACTIVITIES TO DEVELOP THE COMPONENT
1
Cardiovascular       
Endurance
Walking, jogging, aerobics,
Sprint-jog-sprint, shuttle runs, swimming, cycling ...
2
Muscular strength
Lifting, pushing, tag of war, arm curls...
3
Muscular endurance
Running,  swimming, jogging, sit ups, pushups…
4
Flexibility
Sit and reach, back extension, body sideways...
5
Body Composition
Walking, running, jogging, cycling, skipping, sit ups…
The teacher to consider the table above to help learners identify physical activities that can im­
prove Health-Related Fitness during practical activities.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
4
 Learning activity
 
 Discussing the benefits of health-related activities. (Physical Health, mental and 
emotional health, health promotion and disease prevention…)
Learners Should:
Task 1
In groups, discuss the benefits of health-related activities. 
(Physical Health, mental and emotional health, disease prevention…)
CONTENT TIP
     
Below are some of the advantages of engaging in regular health-related activities:
Physical Health
 
 Improved Cardiovascular Health: Regular activities like walking, running, and 
cycling   strengthen the heart, improve blood circulation, and lower the risk of heart 
diseases.
 
 Enhanced Strength and Endurance: Strength training exercises build muscle 
mass, improve endurance, and enable the body to handle physical demands more 
effectively.
 
 Better Weight Management: Physical activities help burn calories, maintain a 
healthy weight, and reduce the risk of obesity-related conditions like diabetes.
 
 Increased Flexibility and Balance: Exercises such as yoga and stretching improve 
flexibility, coordination, and balance, which are essential for preventing injuries 
and falls.
 
 Boosted Immune System: Moderate physical activity helps strengthen the immune 
system, making the body more resilient to illnesses.
 
 Improved Mental Health: While primarily physical, activities also release endorphins 
that reduce stress, anxiety, and depression.
 
 Enhanced Bone and Joint Health: Weight-bearing exercises strengthen bones and 
joints, reducing the risk of osteoporosis and arthritis as one ages.
 
 Higher Energy Levels: Engaging in regular exercise increases energy and stamina, 
making it easier to tackle daily tasks.
Mental and Emotional Benefits
 
 Reduced Stress and Anxiety: Physical activity releases endorphins, which help 
alleviate stress and anxiety.
 
 Improved Mood: Regular exercise has been shown to reduce symptoms of depression 
and improve overall mental well-being.
 
 Enhanced Cognitive Function: Physical activity improves blood flow to the brain, 
boosting cognitive function and reducing the risk of age-related cognitive decline.
 
 Better Sleep: Regular exercise helps regulate sleep patterns, leading to improved 
sleep quality and duration.
Health promotion:

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
5
 
 Encourages a Healthy Lifestyle: Regular physical activity motivates individuals to 
adopt other beneficial habits, such as balanced eating, proper hydration, and stress 
management.
 
 Improves Overall Well-being: Engaging in health-related activities promotes 
physical, mental, and emotional well-being, fostering a more balanced and fulfilling 
life.
 
 Prevents Chronic Illnesses: Physical activities reduce the risk of conditions like heart 
disease, diabetes, and hypertension by improving bodily functions and maintaining 
healthy weight.
 
 Supports Community Health: Participation in group activities, sports, or community 
wellness programs promotes social interactions and collective health awareness.
 
 Strengthens Mental Resilience: Exercise helps combat stress, anxiety, and 
depression, fostering a healthier mental state to cope with life’s challenges.
 
 Boosts Productivity and Energy: Active individuals are more likely to be energized 
and productive, both at work and in personal endeavors.
 
 Strengthens Mental Resilience: Exercise helps combat stress, anxiety, and 
depression, fostering a healthier mental state to cope with life’s challenges.
Disease prevention:
 
 Lower Risk of Chronic Illnesses: Regular physical activity reduces the chances of 
developing conditions such as heart disease, Type 2 diabetes, and hypertension by 
improving heart health, blood sugar regulation, and overall body function.
 
 Strengthened Immune System: Moderate exercise boosts the immune system, 
helping the body defend against infections and illnesses more effectively
 
 Healthy Weight Management: Maintaining a healthy weight through exercise 
decreases the risk of obesity-related diseases like diabetes and certain types of 
cancer.
 
 Improved Mental Health: Activities such as yoga or walking alleviate stress and 
reduce the likelihood of stress-related disorders, including depression and anxiety.
 
 Enhanced Bone and Joint Health: Regular movement strengthens bones and joints, 
reducing the risk of osteoporosis and arthritis.
 
 Prevention of Respiratory Problems: Aerobic exercises improve lung capacity and 
respiratory function, lowering the risk of chronic respiratory diseases.
 
 Social Benefits
 
 Social Connections: Engaging in group fitness activities or sports can help build 
social connections and reduce feelings of loneliness.
 
 Teamwork and Communication: Team sports and activities promote teamwork, 
communication, and problem-solving skills.
 
 Community Engagement: Participating in local fitness events or sports teams can 
help individuals connect with their community and build a sense of belonging.
 
TIP
       
The teacher can include other benefits and explain to the learners so that they can have 
enough knowledge.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
6
 Learning activity
 
 Performing various health related activities. (Endurance, body composition, 
flexibility, strength…). (Jogging, tag of war, stretching Routine…).               
Learners Should:
Task 1
Perform various health related activities. (Endurance, body composition, flexibility, 
strength…).  (Jogging, tag of war, stretching Routine…).
The following are some of the activities that are designed to measure fitness…
ACTIVITY:
Step 1: Two tests are used to measure cardiovascular endurance:
1.	 The Cooper 12-minute run test
 
 This test involves running as fast as possible in 12 minutes. The further you can 
run, the high your level of cardiovascular endurance.
 
 The teacher can let the learners run around the run running truck for 12 minutes 
and observe how many laps each covered in that given time. 
TIP
The teacher may make adjustments to the standard schedule while still ensuring 
successful competence).
2.	 Kasch-Boyer step test
The step test is a simple test requiring a bench or chair 30cm high. 
 
 You step on the bench with both feet and step down again at a pace of once every 
two seconds for three minutes. 
 
 Five seconds after the exercise, your pulse rate is taken for one minute. The lower 
your pulse rate, the higher the level of your cardiovascular endurance.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
7
TIP
Learners to perform according to their abilities and guided by the teacher 
Step 2: Flexibility can be assessed through;
Sit and Reach test
 
 The sit and reach test measures your ability to stretch the muscles of the lower back 
and the back of the legs (hamstrings). 
 
 Sit on the floor with legs straight and feet flat against a base. Reach as far as possi­
ble towards your toes and hold for three seconds.
Arm and Shoulder reach
 
 This exercise measures your ability to stretch the muscles of the shoulders.
 
 Lie face down on the floor with arms straight. 
 
 Hold a stick with your hands shoulder width apart, raise your arms as high as pos­
sible and hold for 3 seconds.
 
 Keep your chin on the floor during the stretch.
Trunk extension test
 
 This test measures your ability to stretch the muscles of your upper back. 
 
 Lie face down on the floor, put your hands behind your head and raise your head 
and shoulders as high as possible holding for 3 seconds.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
8
Expected standards
Health related activities performed correctly.
Sub-Topic: 1.5.2 Isotonic activities
Introduction: 
Isotonic activities are a type of exercise that involves muscle contractions and movements, 
where the muscle length and tension change simultaneously. In other words, isotonic activities 
require the muscles to contract and move the joints through a range of motion, while maintaining 
constant tension. Examples of isotonic exercises include weightlifting, resistance band, exercises, 
bodyweight exercises like push-ups and squats.
Learners will be able to describe the characteristics, benefits and safety guidelines of isotonic 
activities and be able to perform various exercises during the practical lessons.
Specific Competence(s) 
1.5.2.1 Demonstrate isotonic activities.
CONTENT TIP
:     
This lesson will be taught in a natural environment (playing grounds…). The teacher 
can also teach the learners from any of the environments such as Artificial (Modified 
classroom…) and Technological (video clips…)
Teaching materials/ resources
Resistant bands or light weights, yoga mats, dumb bells, medicine balls, benches, (Lo­
cal materials water bottles/ small bags filled with sand/beans, ropes…).
 Learning Activities:
 
 Describing the characteristics of isotonic activities. (Muscle contraction, joint 
movement, constant tension, dynamic movement)

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
9
DISCUSSION
Learners should: 
Task 1
 Describe the characteristics of isotonic activities (Muscle contraction, joint move­
ment, constant tension, dynamic movement).
CONTENT TIP
  
Muscle Contraction
 
 Isotonic activities involve the contraction of muscles, which generates force to move 
the joints.
Movement
 
 Isotonic activities require movement of the joints, which involves the lengthening 
and shortening of muscles.
Constant Tension
 
 Isotonic activities maintain constant tension in the muscles throughout the range of 
motion.
Dynamic movement
 
 Isotonic activities also involve exercises that deal with active, controlled motion 
through a full range of joint movement with muscles contracting and lengthening 
against resistance
 Learning activity
 
 Explaining the benefits of isotonic activities. (Reduce stress, better body posture, 
increased muscle strength…).
Learners should
Task 1
Explain the benefits of isotonic activities individually and in small groups.
CONTENT TIP
    
Improved muscular strength and endurance
 
 Isotonic exercises help build muscular strength and endurance by challenging muscles 
to work against resistance
Improved joint mobility and flexibility
 
 Isotonic exercises can help improve joint mobility and flexibility by increasing range 
of motion and reducing stiffness.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
10
Improved Body Composition
 
 Isotonic exercises can help reduce body fat percentage and increase lean muscle 
mass, leading to improved body composition.
Improved Mental Health and Mood
 
 Isotonic exercises can release endorphins, also known as “feel-good” hormones, 
which can help improve mental health and mood.
Better Sleep Quality
 
 Regular isotonic exercise can help improve sleep quality and duration by reducing 
stress and anxiety.
TIP
Teacher should explain other benefits for the learners to comprehend.
 Learning activity
 
 Performing various isotonic exercises.    (Walking, climbing stairs, Push-up…).
Learners should
Task 1
Perform various isotonic exercises in smaller. groups
CONTENT TIP
      
The teacher should be able to demonstrate and allow learners to perform different iso­
tonic exercises. 
 
 Push-ups are an example of an isotonic exercise, as they involve muscle contraction, 
movement and constant tension throughout the range of motion.
 
 Biceps/dumbbell/arm) curls are also examples of isotonic exercises, as they involve 
muscle contraction, movement and constant tension throughout the range of motion.
TIP
The teacher can include other isotonic activities for the learners to perform.
Sub-Topic 1.5.3 Isometric Activities.
Introduction: 
Isometric activities, also known as isometric exercises or isometrics, are a type of physical 
activity where the muscles contract and generate force without moving the joints. In other words, 
the muscle length and joint angle remain constant, while the muscle tension changes.
Isometric activities involve the static contraction of muscles, where the muscle fibers shorten 
and then maintain a constant length. This type of contraction helps to improve muscular strength, 
endurance, and tone.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
11
Learners will explore the fundamentals of isometric activities, understanding the benefits, types 
and applications in Physical Education. Through a combination of theoretical and practical 
lessons, learners will develop their knowledge, skills and appreciation for isometric exercises.
Specific Competence(s) 
1.5.3.1 Perform isometric activities.
CONTENT TIP
The lesson will be conducted in a natural environment (playing grounds…). The teacher 
can also teach the learners from any of the environments such as Artificial (Modified 
classroom…) and Technological (video clips, shows...)
Teaching material/resources:
Resistance bands, light weights, dumb bells, exercise balls, yoga mats, hand grips or 
stress balls. (Local materials; plastic bottles, ropes…)…
 Learning Activity:
 
 Describing the characteristics of isometric activities.(Muscle contraction, no joint 
movement, constant tension, static movement)
DISCUSSION
Learners should: 
Task 1
Describe the characteristics of isometric activities in pairs and as a class.
CONTENT TIP
   
No Joint Movement
 
 In isometric activities, “no joint movement” refers to the fact that the joints do not 
move or change position during the exercise. The joint angle remains constant, and 
there is no visible movement of the joint.
 
 Isometric activities involve no movement of the joints. The joints remain static and 
the muscles contract without changing the joint angle.
Muscle Contraction
 
 Isometric activities involve the contraction of muscles. The muscle fibers shorten and 
then maintain a constant length.
Constant Muscle Length
 
 During isometric activities, the muscle length remains constant. The muscles contract, 
but the length of the muscle does not change.
Increased Muscle Tension
 
 Isometric activities involve an increase in muscle tension. The muscles contract and

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
12
generate force, which increases muscle tension.
Static Contraction
 
 Isometric activities involve static contractions, where the muscle contracts and then 
holds the contraction for a period.
 Learning activity
 
 Explaining the benefits of isometric activities. (Injury rehabilitation, muscle 
endurance…).
Learners should
Task 2
In groups explain the benefits of isometric activities. (Injury rehabilitation, muscle 
endurance…).
CONTENT TIP
       
Isometric exercises, offer numerous benefits for the body and mind. Some of the 
advantages of incorporating isometric activities into your fitness routine:
Increased Strength: 
 
 Isometric exercises help build strength without moving your joints, which can be 
especially beneficial for people with joint problems or injuries.
Improved Muscle Endurance:
 
 Isometric activities enhance muscle endurance, allowing you to sustain contractions 
for longer periods.
Injury Rehabilitation: 
 
 Isometric exercises are often used in physical therapy to help recover injuries, 
especially those affecting the joints.
CONTENT TIP
Teacher should go in details to give more benefits of isometric activities and allow 
learners to debate.
 Learning activity
 
 Performing various isometric activities. (Pushing the wall, crouch position, scrum…)
Learners should
Task 1
ACTIVITY:
Perform various isometric exercises during a practical lesson:

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
13
TIP
The teacher should be able to demonstrate and allow learners to perform different 
isometric exercises. 
CONTENT TIP
Pushing the wall:  
 
 This combination of muscle contraction without joint movement makes pushing the 
wall an isometric activity. It’s an effective way to build strength, endurance, and 
muscle tone, especially in the upper body.Wall Sit: 
 
 The knee joint remains at a 90-degree angle, and there is no visible movement of the 
joint.
Plank: 
 
 The elbow joint remains at a 90-degree angle, and there is no visible movement of 
the joint.
Glute Bridge: 
 
 The hip joint remains static, and there is no visible movement of the joint.
Expected Standard
Isometric activities performed correctly.
ASSESSMENT CRITERIA
1.	 Multiple-choice quizzes
 
z Accuracy (correct selection of answers). 
 
z time management (complete tasks within the given time) 
 
z understanding of concepts (reflecting knowledge of concepts)
2.	 Short-answer questions
 
z Relevance (answers directly addressing the question
 
z Completeness (include all necessary points and explanations)
 
z Accuracy (correct information and use of terminologies.)
3.	 Essay questions
 
z Content and relevance (address the topic with accurate and relevant details)
 
z Organization (clear introduction, body and conclusion)
 
z Depth of analysis (shows critical thinking and understanding)
 
z Examples and support (includes appropriate example and evidence)
 
z Language and mechanics (grammar, punctuation, spelling and expression.)
4.	 Group project, Research and Field trip reports

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
14
 
z Collaboration 
and 
contribution 
(equal 
participation, 
teamwork 
and 
communication.)
 
z Creativity and initiative (original ideas and thoughtful presentations.)
 
z Presentation, if oral (clear, confident and well-coordinated)
5.	 Class participation and engagement (Debates)
 
z Preparation (demonstrate research and readiness)
 
z Active participation (engaged, contributes meaningfully)
 
z Critical thinking (presents logical and relevant arguments) 
 
z Communication skills (speak clearly, listens actively)
 
z Respect and collaboration (maintains respectful dialogues and good teamwork.
6.	 Practical
 
z Skill execution (performs task with correct technique and form)
 
z Application of theory (shows understanding of rules)
 
z Safety and discipline (follows instructions and uses equipment safely)
 
z Participation and effort (actively involved and shows enthusiasm)
 
z Progress or improvement (demonstrates learning overtime.)
SUMMARY
          
 
 Fitness involves physical activities and exercises that enhance and maintain overall 
health and well-being. It encompasses various aspects such as cardiovascular 
endurance, muscular strength, flexibility, and body composition.
 
 Isotonic Activities are exercises that involve a constant amount of muscle tension and 
movement. During isotonic exercises, muscles change length as they contract and 
move through a range of motion. 
 
 Isometric Activities are exercises where muscles generate force without changing 
length. In these activities, the muscle tension increases without any visible movement.
 
 Incorporating both isotonic and isometric exercises into a fitness routine can help 
improve overall strength, endurance, and muscle definition. Isotonic activities are 
great for building muscle and improving cardiovascular health, while isometric 
activities are effective for strengthening specific muscle groups and increasing 
muscular endurance. Together, they create a well-rounded approach to fitness.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
15
TOPIC: 6 GYMNASTICS
 INTRODUCTION
Gymnastics are sports involving the performance of exercises requiring strength, flexibility, 
balance, coordination, agility endurance and control. The activities form an important part in 
Physical Education and make a significant contribution to the set goals.
Gymnastics involves systematic and rhythmic exercises on the floor (ground), bars and apparatus. 
They define activities which arise when a performer strives to test his/her ability to control the 
body movement. The performance of gymnastics skills is very subjective because it is practical 
without the use of any designed measurement.  The results will therefore entirely depend upon 
human judgment and interpretation.
General Competences
Learners should acquire the following general competencies:
Analytical thinking: Ability to observe, navigate, analyze, design, judge and depict objects 
either natural or artificial.
Emotional intelligence: Ability to demonstrate sports etiquette, express self-awareness, 
self-management and build relationships with others.
Communication: Ability to effectively convey ideas, thoughts, and information through verbal 
and non-verbal means.
Critical Thinking: Capacity to analyze information, evaluate evidence, and form informed 
opinions and decisions.
Problem-Solving: Ability to identify problems, generate solutions, and implement effective 
strategies.
Teamwork and Collaboration: Ability to work effectively with others, build strong relation­
ships, and contribute to team goals.
Adaptability and Flexibility: Capacity to adapt to changing situations, priorities, and environ­
ments.
TERMINOLOGY
Agility: 	
Ability to move quickly and with accuracy
Endurance: 	 Ability to sustain prolonged physical activity or effort over an extended period of 
time.
Flexibility:	
Range of motion in a person’s joints and muscles.
Gymnastics:	 Sports involving the performance of exercises requiring strength, flexibility, 
                       balance and control
Roll: 	 	
To move with repeated turning or rotating motion.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
16
Spotting: 	
Practice of assisting a gymnast during a skill or routine to ensure their safety. 
Stunts:   	
Skills or acrobatic routines that are performed on various apparatus.
Tumbling:	
Acrobatic movements performed on the ground or a tumbling track.
Vault: 	
To leap or spring over something, especially by pushing on it with the hands or 
using a pole.
Sub-topic:  1.6.1. Stunts and Tumbling activities.	
Introduction.
This sub-topic specifically looks at Stunts and tumbling activities which refer to the skills 
performed that often involve lifting or supporting another gymnast and acrobatic movements 
performed on the ground or a tumbling track respectively. 
Both stunts and tumbling are important to routines in gymnastics and are judged based on 
difficulty, execution and overall presentation. Through them, dedication, perseverance, 
resourcefulness, self-confidence and courage are developed. Gymnastic activities also contribute 
to overall physical fitness.
Specific competence(s):

TOPIC: 1.7 RECREATIONAL GAMES AND ACTIVITIES
INTRODUCTION  
Recreational games are a structured form of play, usually undertaken for enjoyment, relaxation, 
and sometimes competition. They can be physical, mental, or a combination of both, and are 
always played according to specific rules. Recreational games and activities refer to those played 
primarily for fun, fitness, relaxation, and social interaction rather than for serious competition. 
They are an important part of physical education and holistic development, providing opportunities 
for learners to engage in enjoyable movement-based experiences.
These activities play a vital role in promoting physical fitness, mental well-being, and social 
development. They serve as a stress reliever and create an engaging way for individuals to stay 
active and healthy. Recreational games also help foster essential life skills such as teamwork, 
leadership, communication and strategic thinking.
Recreational activities are highly inclusive, catering to diverse interests, ages, and ability 
levels. They may include aerobic games (like dance, tag, or relay races…), traditional games 
(often rooted in cultural heritage), and group activities that promote cooperation and emotional 
intelligence. In educational settings, these games help create a dynamic learning environment 
where learners not only exercise but also express themselves creatively and socially.
General competences: 
Learners should acquire the following general competencies:
Analytical thinking: Ability to observe, navigate, analyse, design, judge and depict objects 
either natural or artificial.
Emotional intelligence: ability to demonstrate sports etiquette, express self-awareness, self-
management and build relationships with others.
Communication: Ability to effectively convey ideas, thoughts, and information through verbal 
and non-verbal means.
Critical Thinking: Capacity to analyze information, evaluate evidence, and form informed 
opinions and decisions.
Problem-Solving: Ability to identify problems, generate solutions, and implement effective 
strategies.
 Teamwork and Collaboration: Ability to work effectively with others, build strong relationships, 
and contribute to team goals.
Adaptability and Flexibility: Capacity to adapt to changing situations, priorities, and 
environments.
TERMINOLOGY
Aerobics: Exercises that combine rhythmic activities with stretching and strength training 
routines
Aerobic Activity: Any physical activity that increases the heart rate and promotes cardiovascular 
health (e.g., jogging, skipping, dance games).

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
22
Beat: Underlying rhythm of music
Culture: Shared beliefs, values, customs and behaviors of a group of people. 
Game: Any activity or sport that involves structured play, often with rules and objectives. 
Meter: Rhythmic structure of music. 
Recreational: Activities that are done for enjoyment, relaxation and leisure.
Rhythm: pattern of sounds and silences in music or speech.
Rules: Specific guidelines that dictate how a game is played
Tempo: Speed pace at which a piece of music is played or performed. 
Sub-topic: 1.7.1. Aerobics. 	
Introduction:
Aerobic dances refer to rhythmic body movements with or without music following a certain 
rhythmic pattern. Aerobics can be non-loco motor or locomotor, and can be done individually, 
in pairs or in groups. They can be accompanied by music or routine counts and claps. Jumps 
and swings can also be included. In aerobics, rhythm is the basis of music and dance. It plays a 
major role in our lives. In order to respond very well to the rhythmic dancing styles, one has to 
be physically fit.
Specific competence(s): 
1.7.1.1. Perform step and rhythmic patterns of aerobics
TIP
The teacher can teach the learners from any of the environments such as nature 
(playing fields, hall, room or any open space…), Artificial (Modified classroom, …) 
and Technological (video clips, shows...)
Teaching/learning resources:
Step Platforms, Workout Mats, Proper Footwear, Music System, Fitness Tracker, 
hoops, ropes, resistance bands, dumb bells, cones markers… (locally improvised mate­
rials; fiber, bottles, self-made balls bean balls…)
 Learning activity:
 
 Demonstrating step and rhythmic patterns of aerobics.
(Rhythmic patterns and steps, legs and arms stretch, sidekicks, neck, roll…).
DISCUSSION
Learners should:
Task: 1
In groups, explore various sources of rhythmic accompaniment, their unique 
characteristics, and the features of rhythmic backgrounds.

Teaching Module
23
CONTENT TIP
	
Guide the learners that they can learn both the music and the words, allowing them to 
provide their own accompaniment. 
 
 Dance drum.
 
 Record player.

 Cassette player.
 
 Piano.
 
 Tape recorder.
 
 Tambourine.
 
 Rhythm bands.
 
 Guitar.

 Rattles, 
Characteristics of rhythmic background
CONTENT TIP
Learners to recognise, understand and appreciate the music characteristics which are 
also present in other purely rhythmic accompaniments which include: 
Tempo
 
 This is the speed of the music. It can be constant or show a gradual increase or 
decrease.
Beat
 
 This is the underlying rhythm of music. Sometime it is referred to as the pulse of 
the music. Music with pronounced beat is easier to follow. 
Meter
 
 This refers to the manner in which the beats are put together to form a measure of 
music. It is typically noted at the beginning of a piece with a time signature which 
consists of two numbers. Common meters include: -  , ,   and   .
CONTENT TIP
In smaller groups, learners should perform various rhythmic patterns of aerobics while 
applying the three fundamentals of rhythm.
 
 Locomotor movements: These are rhythmic movements that enable an individual to 
move from one place to another. Examples include walking, running, jumping, hopping, 
skipping and leaping.
 
 Non-locomotor movements: These are rhythmic movements that are performed 
without changing location, only body parts are the ones that move. Examples include 
bending, stretching, twisting, swaying and balancing.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
24
 
 Manipulative movements. These movements involve the control and movement of 
objects using the body or handling objects. Examples include actions like throwing, 
catching, kicking dribbling and striking
Expected standard 
Step and rhythmic patterns of aerobics performed accurately.
Sub-topic:  1.7.2. Traditional games. 	
Introduction:
Traditional games are activities passed down through generations, associated with specific 
ethnic groups or societies. They often feature simple rules, require minimal equipment, and have 
unknown origins or discoverers.
Traditional games serve various purposes, including entertainment, socialisation and education. 
They can be played indoors or outdoors and often involve physical activity, teamwork and strategy. 
Examples include games like tag, hide and seek, and various board games that reflect the 
customs and value of a particular culture. Traditional games not only provide fun but also help in 
preserving cultural identity and fostering community bonds.
Specific competence: 
1.7.2.1. Perform traditional games
TIP
The teacher can also teach the learners from any of the environments such as Natural 
(playing grounds…), Artificial (Modified classroom…) and Technological (video clips, 
shows...)
Teaching/learning resources:
Skipping ropes, Chalk for marking, cones, Drums, Clapping or rhythm sticks, 
 Learning activity:
 
 Performing traditional games. (Jumping games, Rhythmic, Manipulative, break-
through…).
ACTIVITY:
Learners should:
Task 1: 
Research and perform the indigenous Zambian Traditional games that are practiced or 
played in their various communities.
Chasing games.
 
 Chain tag game…
Breakthrough games

Teaching Module
25
 
 Sheep-sheep come all…
Rhythmic Games. 
 
 Musical Chairs: Players walk around a circle of chairs while music plays. When 
the music stops, learner must find a chair to sit in. The player left standing is out. 
…
Knowledge testing games.
 
 Who Am I? Players guess the name of a famous figure or concept using hints 
provided by others, …

 A variety of Zambian manipulative and calculative games to be researched and 
discussed
Task 2
Highlight the importance of Zambian Traditional Games.
CONTENT TIP
In smaller groups learners to discuss the importance of Zambian Traditional Games.
Preservation of Cultural Heritage
 
 Traditional games are a way of passing down customs, values and stories from one 
generation to another.
 
 They reflect Zambia’s rich ethnic diversity and promote pride in indigenous identi­
ties.
 
 They promote, preserve and maintain cultural values in society.
 
 They consolidate historical background of cultures
 
 They also provide and promote acceptance and appreciation of other cultures from 
different backgrounds and regions.
Social Development
 
 Most games are played in groups, promoting teamwork, cooperation, and commu­
nication.
 
 Learners acquire important social skills like turn-taking, fairness, leadership, and 
conflict resolution.
 
 They prevent anti-social behavior and vandalism.
 
 They are a source of enjoyment and fun. 
 
 They act as an agent of socialization in the society 
 
 They build adolescence belief in their ability to influence or resist their peers
Physical Development
 
 Traditional games involving running, jumping, chasing, or balancing help improve 
motor skills, physical fitness, and coordination.
 
 They encourage learners to stay active and healthy through play.
Mental and Cognitive Growth
 
 Traditional games stimulate critical thinking, problem-solving, memory and numer­
acy.
 
 Knowledge-based games enhance language development and creativity.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
26
 
 They help promote self-esteem and confidence in oneself.
Emotional Well-being
 
 Participating in traditional games boosts confidence and self-esteem as learners 
learn to win and lose gracefully.
 
 Traditional games provide a healthy outlet for releasing energy and emotions, reduc­
ing stress and anxiety.
 
 They help to teach good attitudes to learners.
Education through Informal Learning
 
 Traditional games naturally integrate learning into play, making it easier for learners 
to grasp academic and life concepts.
 
 Concepts like counting, strategy, and rhythm are learned in a fun and engaging way.
 
 They act as informal education process.
Inclusion and Accessibility
 
 Traditional games require minimal or no equipment, making them accessible to 
learners in both urban and rural areas.
 
 They often encourage inclusivity, allowing learners of different ages and abilities to 
participate.
Community Bonding
 
 During festivals or communal gatherings, traditional games bring people together 
across generations, reinforcing a sense of belonging and unity.
 
 Traditional games help shape children into responsible and respectful adults by 
teaching important values such as honesty, patience, perseverance, discipline and 
respect for others. 
Expected Standard- 
Zambian Traditional games performed correctly.

promote fitness and social interaction. 
 
 Aerobics involves rhythmic exercises performed to music, focusing on

 They include various styles like step aerobics or dance aerobics. 
 
 Traditional games are cultural activities passed down through generations, often

TOPIC: 1.8  SWIMMING
INTRODUCTION
Swimming refers to the act of moving through water using coordinated movements of the arms 
and legs typically employing specific strokes. It can also describe gliding or floating effortlessly 
on the water’s surface. Throughout history, swimming has been an important survival skill and a 
valuable recreational and competitive activity.
In modern times, swimming is widely recognized for its many benefits. It provides a full-body 
workout, improving cardiovascular endurance, muscle strength, flexibility and coordination. 
Whether enjoyed for fun with friends and family or pursued as a competitive sport, swimming 
promotes both physical and mental well-being.
This topic focuses on introducing learners to the fundamentals of swimming with special attention 
to water safety practices and acceptable pool hygiene.
General Competencies to be attained by learners: 
Creativity and Innovation: Applying knowledge and skills in new and creative ways.
Problem-Solving: Tackling real-life issues using various strategies.
Collaboration: Appreciating and acknowledging group members’ views while participating in 
activities.
Communication: Using appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different situations.
Analytical Thinking: Observing, analyzing and evaluating situations for better judgment.
Environmental Sustainability: Learning environmental ethics and adopting protection 
strategies.
TERMINOLOGY
 
 Aquatic: anything related to water.
 
 Buoyancy: the force that allows objects to float in a liquid like water.
 
 Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR): A lifesaving technique to restore blood 
circulation and breathing in someone who has stopped breathing or whose heart has 
stopped.
 
 Conscious: Being aware and able to think about one’s surroundings, thoughts, and 
emotions.
 
 Micro-organisms: Tiny living organisms often too small to be seen with the naked 
eye.
 
 Rescuer: A person who saves someone from a dangerous or harmful situation.
 
 Rip Currents: Powerful, fast-moving waves of water that flow from the shore to deep­
er waters.
 
 Scenario: An imagined or planned situation that describes a particular setting.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
29
 
 Submerging: Completely sinking something under the water’s surface.
 
 Swimming: Moving through water by using limbs or floating.
 
 Waterborne Illnesses: Diseases caused by harmful microorganisms in contaminated 
water.
Sub-Topic: 1.8.1 Water Safety 
Introduction: 
Water safety refers to the practices, precautions and awareness needed to prevent accidents, 
injuries or drowning in aquatic environments. Participating in water activities requires personal 
responsibility, respect for safety guidelines and an understanding of water conditions. Whether 
in a swimming pool, lake, river or ocean, being careless or unaware of basic safety rules can lead 
to serious consequences.
Specific Competence: 
1.8.1.1 Apply water safety practices.
TIP
The teacher can teach the learners from any of the environments such as Natural (class­
room…), Artificial (Swimming pool…) and Technological (video clips, simulators...)
Teaching/learning materials:
Charts and diagrams, markers and digital devices. Instructional videos, goggles, 
swimming caps floatation props, first aid kit, whistle… (locally improvised materials; 
plastic bottle sealed with lids tied together to create float aids, empty containers, banana 
barks, dried calabash…)  
 Learning Activity:
 
 Creating simulated water safety scenario. (Mock pool emergency…)
ACTIVITY:
Learners Should:
Task 1: simulate a pool emergency scenario.
Setup:
 
 Arrange towels on the floor to outline a “pool” area.
 
 Place floatation devices nearby.
 
 Take up roles as assigned by the teacher.
 
 Create a scenario where a swimmer appears to be drowning or in distress.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
30
TIP
Teacher to give the following roles to learners and guide them through the scenario
 
 Lifeguard must observe, identify the emergency, blow the whistle, and perform an 
appropriate rescue using a floatation aid — without jumping in.
 
 Onlookers must react realistically: panic, call for help, or try to assist (safely).
 
 Emergency responders must be “called” and arrive to provide further assistance, 
including CPR or first aid if needed.
 
 Swimmer in distress/victim should act as though they are struggling in water 
(safely dramatized).
 
 Guide the scenario step-by-step.
 
 Pause the activity at key moments to ask questions like:
 
z What did the lifeguard do correctly?
 
z Did anyone enter the water unnecessarily?
 
z How could onlookers have helped more safely?
 
 Encourage reflection and improvement after each run-through.
Task 2: Scenario Execution 
In groups, act out roles as assigned in task 1.
 
 Lifeguard:
 
 Quickly notices the emergency.
 
 Assesses the situation.
 
 Gives clear verbal commands to onlookers (Call for help! Stay back!).
 
 Uses a floatation device to assist the swimmer without entering the water, unless 
absolutely necessary.
 
 Swimmer in Distress / Victim:
 
 Acts out the chosen emergency realistically (but safely).
 
 May show signs of panic, confusion, unconsciousness, or cramping.
 
 Onlookers
 
 React realistically (some may panic; others may be helpful).
 
 Listen to the lifeguard and follow instructions (calling emergency services, 
bringing a floatation device).
 
 Emergency Responders:
 
 Arrive on the scene when called.
 
 Simulate first aid or CPR if needed.
 
 Talk through their actions clearly (check for breathing and call for medical 
personnel.)

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
31
CONTENT TIP
 
 Learning Activity:
 
 Researching and presenting about different water safety topics. (Rip currents, 
pool safety, water-borne illnesses…).  
ACTIVITY:
Learners should:
Task 1
In small groups, conduct a research, identify key facts, risks and prevention strategies related on 
the following topics as assigned by the teacher
 
 Rip currents
 
 pool safety
 
 water-borne illnesses …
Each group will create a visual aid (Poster/slideshow) and prepare a brief presentation.
CONTENT TIPS
Rip currents: 
 
 These are powerful, fast-moving channels of water that flow from the shore to deep 
water. 
 
 They appear as darker, uneven water with a noticeable difference in wave pattern. 
 
 They often pull swimmers away from the shore because they flow from the shore 
toward deeper water in a narrow fast-moving current.
Pool safety:
 
 Use pool covers when the pool is not in use to prevent accidental falls or drownings.
 
 Limit the use of alcohol or drugs while swimming.
 
 All individuals, especially children at an early age must learn to swim to increase 
safety in the water.
 
 Know how to administer Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation 
 
 Regularly keep the pool clean and well maintained to prevent accidents
 
 Shallow and deep end safety must be marked clearly so as to prevent swimmers 
jumping or diving in shallow water.
Waterborne illnesses:
 
 These are diseases caused by harmful microorganisms (bacteria, viruses and 
parasites) found in contaminated water which can lead to Bilharzia, typhoid, 
dysentery, cholera…   
 
 These can occur when people swim, drink or come into contact with contaminated 
water, especially in pools, lakes, rivers or even oceans.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
32
 Learning Activity:
 
 Demonstrating water safety practices. (Use of life jacket, walking safely, hygiene, 
depth of the pool, Life-saving practices: breathing …). 
ACTIVITY:
Learners should:
Task 1: 
Depth of the pool awareness 
 
 Set-up a “depth quiz” with colored markers such as red for shallow, blue for deep 
where learners will match activities like diving or wading to the correct zones.
 
 In groups, do an audit of what the school swimming pool (if available) has and what 
it does not have. Write a brief report of what should be done to make it an ideal safe 
place for learning how to swim and what should be put in place before swimming 
starts. Present the findings and suggestions.
 
 If the school does not have a swimming pool, groups can do a theoretical audit or 
watch a video showing a swimming pool, then discuss what an ideal and safe swim­
ming pool should have.
Task 2: 
Practice Life-saving skills 
Rescue scenarios: 
 
 Practice “Reach, Throw, Don’t Go” methods using poles, ropes or floatation devices 
to assist a struggling swimmer without entering the water.
Cardio-Pulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) Practices: 
 
 Perform chest compressions and rescue breathing on a fellow learner or training 
model. 
Passive drowning response: 
 
 Role-play rescuing an unconscious swimmer, securing them face-up and towing 
them to safety. 
CONTENT TIPS
Pool depth awareness
 
 Identify shallow and deep ends using tiles, signs or ropes
 
 Practice entering feet first in shallow water and using ladders or steps
 
 Test floating in different depths to understand how buoyancy varies.
Expected Standard: Water safety practices applied correctly.
Sub-Topic: 1.8.2 Pool 
Introduction: 
A pool is a specially built area filled with water where people swim, play or learn water skills. 
Pools can be found in schools, homes or public places and they come in different shapes, sizes 
and depths to suit different activities and age groups.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
33
To keep the pool safe and enjoyable for everyone, pool hygiene and good practices are very 
important. This means keeping the water clean, avoiding littering, showering before entering the 
pool and not swimming when sick. Safe behavior like walking instead of running near the pool 
and following pool rules also helps prevent accidents and protects the health of all swimmers.
Specific Competence(s): 
1.8.2.1 Practice pool hygiene.
CONTENT TIPS
The teacher can teach the learners from any of the environments such as Natural 
(classroom…), Artificial (Swimming pool…) and Technological (video clips, 
simulators...)
 Learning Activity:
 
 Practicing Pool Hygiene: (Avoid spitting, take a shower before entering the pool 
and avoid urinating or defecating in the water…).
ACTIVITY:
Learners Should:
Task: 1
In smaller groups identify good pool practices, design posters highlighting key rules on 
pool hygiene and display them as guided by the teacher.
CONTENT TIPS
 
 Never swim immediately after a heavy meal. Wait at least 30 minutes after a light meal, 
or more after a heavy meal. 
 
 Always shower before swimming. For sanitary reasons it is very important for a person 
to take a shower before entering the pool. It is also advised to shower after swimming.
 
 Always put on the correct swimming costume.
 
 Water must always be kept clean by not spitting, urinating, or defecating in the water
 
 Not to chew gum or anything while swimming. 
 
 Not to eat or drink in the pool premises.
 Learning activity
 
 Debating Pool Hygiene Practices (pool cleaning, maintenance…)
Learners Should:
Task: 1 
Debate for or against the following motions concerning pool hygiene practices
Motions:
1.	 Public swimming pools should implement mandatory hygiene checks before

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
34
opening each day to ensure water safety and cleanliness.
 
z
This motion encourages debate on whether it should be a requirement for 
pools to undergo routine hygiene inspections and checks for cleanliness before 
opening to the public each day.
2.	 Recreational floatation devices should be banned in public swimming pools to 
improve water hygiene and prevent contamination.
 
z
This motion focuses on the pros and cons of banning floatation devices in public 
pools as a measure to reduce contamination and maintain cleaner water.
3.	 learners should be required to wear approved swimming costumes in public pools 
to ensure better hygiene and prevent the spread of germs.
 
z
This motion argues whether it should be mandatory for children to wear specific 
swimming attire designed for hygiene purposes when using public swimming 
pools.
TIP
Teacher to guide learners with other different motions for them to appreciate pool 
hygiene practices.
Expected Standard: Pool hygiene practices performed effectively.

z
Creativity and initiative (original ideas and thoughtful presentations.)
 
z
Presentation, if oral (clear, confident and well-coordinated)
5.	 Class participation and engagement (Debates)
 
z
Preparation (demonstrate research and readiness)

6.	 Practical
 
z
Skill execution (performs task with correct technique and form)

Safety and discipline (follows instructions and uses equipment safely)
 
z
Participation and effort (actively involved and shows enthusiasm)
 
z
Progress or improvement (demonstrates learning overtime.)
SUMMARY
 
 Swimming is a highly beneficial physical activity that offers numerous advantages for 
both the body and mind. Physically, it enhances cardiovascular health, builds muscle 
strength, and improves flexibility. Mentally, it reduces stress, promotes relaxation, 
and boosts overall mood.
 
 Practicing water safety is crucial for preventing accidents and ensuring the well-being 
of individuals in and around water.
 
 Proper pool hygiene is essential for a healthy swimming environment. Maintaining 
good pool hygiene is a shared responsibility between pool owners and swimmers. 
Regular cleaning, chemical treatment and adhering to these practices help prevent 
the spread of waterborne diseases and ensure a safe and pleasant experience for all 
swimmers

HOMEWORK:	
	
Observe different activities being performed by people around you. Analyse how each component 
of health-related fitness is being utilised. Thereafter, write its benefits to the user in relation to 
their physical, mental and social health.
LESSON EVALUATION:
Lesson was successfully taught. Learners were able to identify and discuss health-related fitness 
using available teaching/learning materials such as video clips, charts …. The methods used in 
the lesson were group discussion, question and answer, individual work which stimulated active 
participation. However, some learners needed support on identifying different health-related fit­
ness hence homework was given.
